checks.a_analysis: I have compared the output JSON against the source PDF. Every heading and paragraph in the output matches the text content of the source document precisely. There are no fabricated instructions or descriptions. The metadata title is an AI-generated placeholder which is permitted under the 'Acceptable Changes' criteria.

The source document contains no phone numbers, email addresses, or physical addresses. The output JSON correctly reflects this by not including any such information. All textual content is strictly instructional.

The source document consists of three pages. The output JSON includes sections for all three pages. Page 1 contains the first 4 instructional steps and their associated 4 images. Page 2 contains 3 instructional steps and their 3 associated images. Page 3 contains the final 2 instructional steps and 2 images. All content is accounted for.
The source document contains no tables; it is a series of screenshots with accompanying instructional text. Therefore, this check is not applicable and passes.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: I verified that all instructional sentences from the source are present in the output. No part of any sentence has been truncated or omitted. For example, the detailed instruction for step 5 regarding the network security key is present in its entirety in reading order 10.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: There are minor instances of punctuation normalization (e.g., using a straight apostrophe in 'SSID's' instead of the source's curly 'SSID’s'), but these fall under acceptable reformatting. The meaning of the text is perfectly preserved.

checks.a_analysis: I have reviewed the output JSON against the source document. Each paragraph of instruction and the title appear only once in the JSON, matching their single occurrence in the original PDF. There are no duplicated text blocks.

The output JSON identifies the document title as an H1, which is correct. While subsequent pages/sections do not have their own headings in the JSON, the source document also lacks additional formal headings (it consists of a title and a sequence of steps). The hierarchy is flat but logical for this type of document.
The source document contains no tables, and the output correctly contains no table elements.

The reading order in the output JSON is significantly flawed. In the source document, each text instruction is immediately followed by a corresponding screenshot or icon (e.g., the text 'Click on Computer Icon...' is followed by the icon image). In the output JSON, all paragraphs within a section are grouped together at the beginning (elements 2-5 for Section 1), followed by all images (elements 6-9 for Section 1). This grouping repeats in Section 2 (elements 10-12 then 13-15) and Section 3 (elements 16-17 then 18-19). This separation breaks the logical association between the instructions and the visual aids intended to support them.
checks.b_evidence_source: On page 1, the instruction 'Click on Computer Icon in bottom right of screen to open options' is immediately followed by a small image showing the computer icon.
checks.b_evidence_output: The text element 'Click on Computer Icon...' (reading_order 2) is followed by three other text instructions (reading_order 3, 4, 5) before the corresponding image appears as element 6.

checks.a_analysis: The source document presents instructions as discrete text blocks. The output JSON represents these as paragraphs. While they function as a list of steps, they are not explicitly marked as such in the source, so conversion to paragraphs is an acceptable structural interpretation.
There are no links in the source document, and the output correctly identifies no links.

checks.a_analysis: The source PDF is a step-by-step guide where instructions are immediately followed by their corresponding screenshots. In the converted HTML, the logical visual flow is severely compromised. In the middle section (corresponding to PDF page 2), the text for three separate steps is grouped together first, followed by the images in a scrambled order: Step 6 (Verifying), Step 7 (Portal), then Step 5 (Security Key). Furthermore, the captions associated with these images are mismatched (e.g., the 'Verifying' image is captioned as the 'Security Key' popup). A similar scrambling occurs for the content from PDF page 3, where the 'Success' screen and 'Connected Wi-Fi' list are swapped and mislabeled. This rearrangement prevents a user from easily following the visual flow of the guide.
checks.b_evidence_source: PDF Page 2 shows Step 5 (Security key text + image), then Step 6 (Verifying text + image), then Step 7 (Portal text + image).
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML screenshot shows text for Steps 5, 6, and 7 grouped. Below them, images are sequenced incorrectly: Verifying screen (labeled 'Popup screen for NCGov-Guest network security key'), Portal screen (labeled 'Verifying and connecting screen'), and Security Key screen (labeled 'Secure Internet Portal splash page').
checks.b_evidence_location: Middle and bottom sections of the HTML document, corresponding to content from PDF pages 2 and 3.

checks.a_analysis: The source document does not contain any tables; it is composed of text instructions and UI screenshots.
All meaningful images from the source PDF (icons, UI windows, web portal screens) are present in the HTML output, although their placement and captions are incorrect (as noted in Check V1).

There is no visual duplication of the document's body content. While descriptions of the image content are rendered as captions, this is an accessible representation of the images rather than a duplication of the source text.
